moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the English Opening, one of the most flexible and positional openings in chess. This move immediately stakes a claim on the central d5 square while keeping our options completely open - we can transpose into many different pawn structures depending on how Black responds. The English is beloved by positional players because it often leads to rich middlegame positions where understanding pawn chains, piece coordination, and long-term planning matter more than memorizing sharp tactical lines. By starting with c4 instead of the more direct e4 or d4, we're signaling that we want to build our position slowly and react to whatever setup our opponent chooses.

Black develops the knight to its most natural square, immediately putting pressure on our potential e4 advance and preparing to contest the center. This is the start of the Anglo-Indian Defense, where Black adopts a flexible setup that can transpose into various Indian defenses depending on how we continue. The knight on f6 is perfectly placed - it controls the central e4 and d5 squares while preparing kingside castling. Our opponent is keeping maximum flexibility, just as we did with our opening move, and can still choose between setups like the King's Indian, Nimzo-Indian, or Queen's Indian depending on our next moves.

We develop our knight to its most active square, supporting a future e4 advance while maintaining the flexible character of our position. This move keeps all our options open - we can still play d4 and transpose into various Queen's Pawn openings, or continue with the pure English setup with moves like g3 and Bg2. The knight on f3 also prepares kingside castling and controls important central squares like e5 and d4. By mirroring Black's development, we're staying in the theoretical sweet spot of the English where both sides have numerous good options and the position remains rich with possibilities.

Black chooses the Old Indian formation, creating a solid but somewhat passive pawn structure that supports the pawn on f6 and prepares to fianchetto the dark-squared bishop with g6 and Bg7. This setup is less ambitious than the King's Indian (which would involve e5) but also more solid - Black is prioritizing piece development and king safety over immediate central counterplay. The d6 pawn controls the e5 square and gives the dark-squared bishop a retreat square, while keeping the option of playing e5 later when the position is more developed. We should now consider how to best exploit our slight space advantage, typically through moves like g3, Bg2, and 0-0, building a strong positional foundation before deciding on our central pawn breaks.
